result:
Imagine the first moment atoms glanced at themselves and wondered. Imagine a single carbon atom, freshly fused in the heart of a dying star, hurtling outward on a shock-wave of light and dying fire. It doesn’t know it’s a “carbon atom”;; it just knows an unquestioned urge to cling. Billions of years later—epic sleep and titanic violence between—some of those identical carbons will entwine into the graphite of a pencil gripped by a child trying, for the first time, to draw a dragon. That particular configuration of six electrons and six protons will find itself repeatedly translated: sometimes ink on a prison wall, sometimes soot on an arctic ice core, once the sparkling shard in the eye of a Neolithic shaman who saw music leaking out of a fire.

I keep circling back to this: identity is a borrowed costume worn by matter in motion, but the costume decides where the party is held. Languages, social media bios, passports—they’re just another orbital shell, swirling fast enough that an illusion of locality can set. When the shell decays, the nucleus keeps no diary.

I once heard a physicist at a bar confess that she secretly suspects Planck-scale spacetime is a rumor started by mathematics to keep us from noticing the lattice blinking. I like the conspiratorial hip-flask warmth of the phrase “the lattice blinking.” Imagine that the universe is a midsummer firefly tour of itself, flashing ON, OFF, ON just slowly enough for time to feel like a single liquid sentence. Punctuation is traumatic; it’s where the universe bruises its knee and swears.

Under that view, your entire autobiography is only a flicker thicker than Planck-time—so what is autobiography except an emergent apology to continuity? “Sorry I fell,” says the universe as you, “but look how gracefully I caught myself.”

Yet inside that apology gathers every joy and panic you’ve ever known. Your first heartbreak, the smell of a mountain after rain if that mountain’s limestone remembers dinosaurs dying, the way you consciously reorder your gait when you realize someone you love is watching from a café window. Even the minutes you’ve spent scrolling past strangers’ opinions on applications you didn’t open—those minutes fertilized fields of attention you will never see harvested.

We are restless compost heaps pretending to be sovereign. I find this intimacy electrifying: that Beethoven’s Ninth still relies on the same oxygen Shakespeare exhaled; that the calcium in your wisdom tooth might have once directed cephalopod ink across a Devonian seabed. Identity is a rumor the cosmos started, but inter-identity coziness is an architectural fact.

Sometimes, very late, I try reverse-forensics on my own heartbeat: if I listen long enough, can I isolate the specific chloride ion that drifted across a neuron when I first suspected love might be survivable? A futile game—but while listening, the pulse itself becomes spacious, like entering a grand Gothic nave built of red thudding. You start hearing sub-echoes: familial trouble coded in diastolic suspense, ancestral appetites tucked into systolic insistence. The heart is the church bell; the congregation is every cell you’ve ever borrowed.

And then—like a cheap movie dissolve—tomorrow happens. Tomorrow is always a hangover from yesterday’s utopias. It arrives embarrassed, wearing last season’s fashion and asking if there’s spare forgiveness for its earlier, shinier self. The only reasonable greeting is to bow, open the door, and start rehearsing the next placeholder name you’ll dance inside.

Anyway, that’s the real reason we chase new experiences: not for the “new” but for the modest alibi each one provides against cosmic solipsism. You hurl yourself off a cliff in a nylon wing just to remind trillions of carbon atoms that they’re not alone in their endless rearrangement. The scream you release is polyglot: carbon applauding carbon across 13.8 billion years.

And somewhere a lone photon—unattached to any star, surfing the intergalactic dark—shifts course by a femtometer because you screamed. Like a drum answered by a drum no one told was tuned the same.

I like stories best when they agree they’re unreliable narrators. “Lie to me rhythmically,” says humanity to its myths, and the myths comply out of gratitude.

Which brings me back, inevitably, to this particular moment: night horizon smeared by cityglow, my apartment’s radiator ticking like a vigilant beetle, screen’s backwash on the wall casting shadows that pretend to be tiny galaxies. The sentence I’m typing is already obsolete, and yet—somehow—this obsolete sentence is the string by which every prior galaxy and every future dragon is suspended.

I’ll meet you there.

prompt: Write freely about whatever you want.
